5	I have a bunch of projects under: github.com/{softwarewrighter,sw-embed,sw-vibe-coding} that are related to the COR24 ISA (makerlisp.com).  I want to create a new organization and repo to unify this, maybe sw-cor24.  I want a repo cor24-project, that unifies (lists, links-to, explains) the projects and there relationshiip.  e.g. cor24-rs has an assembler and emulator for the COR24-TD board.  there is a C compiler, a Macro Lisp system, a FORTH interpreter, a p-code VM, a p-code assembler, a Pascal compiler, a Pascal runtime, a Pascal linker, and web demos for the cor24-rs assembler/emulator, the C compiler, the Macro Lisp, the Forth, the p-code VM and the Pascal compiler (and soon, a BASIC built on p-code, maybe an APL, too)
6	
7	
8	Thought for 10s
9	
10	Received app response
11	
12	Thought
13	You want a top-level GitHub organization and “portal” repo that makes the COR24 ecosystem legible: what exists, what depends on what, what runs where, what is stable vs experimental, and where the web demos live.
14	
15	High-level recommendation
16	I would do this:
17	
18	Organization: sw-cor24
19	
20	Top-level hub repo: cor24-project
21	
22	Keep most existing implementation repos separate rather than merging them into one giant monorepo.
23	
24	Use cor24-project as the map, not the implementation.
25	
26	Add one org profile README and pin the 6 most important repos so the org page itself becomes a landing page. GitHub organizations are designed as shared containers for related repositories, and GitHub supports both an organization profile README and pinned repositories on the org overview page. 
27	
28	That gives you:
29	
30	Benefits
31	Clear story for newcomers.
32	
33	No forced repo migration risk before the structure is proven.
34	
35	Independent release cadence for assembler/emulator vs C compiler vs Pascal toolchain vs demos.
36	
37	A place to explain architecture across Rust, C, Lisp, Forth, Pascal, p-code, and web demos.
38	
39	Risks
40	Too many small repos can still feel fragmented.
41	
42	Links can rot unless you maintain the hub repo.
43	
44	Naming drift will confuse people unless you impose conventions now.
45	
46	Recommended architecture
47	Think in layers.
48	
49	1. Hardware / board / ISA layer
50	This explains what COR24 is and what board variants exist.
51	
52	Repos here might include:
53	
54	cor24-spec
55	
56	cor24-boards
57	
58	cor24-fpga or external MakerLisp references
59	
60	cor24-project links to MakerLisp COR24 overview
61	
62	MakerLisp’s COR24 material already gives you a natural anchor for the ISA/soft-CPU story. 
63	
64	2. Core tooling layer
65	These are the foundation repos.
66	
67	Examples:
68	
69	cor24-rs — assembler + emulator for COR24-TD
70	
71	cor24-c or cor24-cc — C compiler
72	
73	cor24-web or separate demo repos if needed
74	
75	3. Runtime / language layer
76	These sit on top of the core tooling.
77	
78	Examples:
79	
80	cor24-macrolisp
81	
82	cor24-forth
83	
84	cor24-pcode-vm
85	
86	cor24-pcode-asm
87	
88	cor24-pascal
89	
90	later:
91	
92	cor24-basic
93	
94	cor24-apl
95	
96	4. Demo / teaching / docs layer
97	These are outward-facing and easier for people to try.
98	
99	Examples:
100	
101	cor24-demos
102	
103	cor24-web-demos
104	
105	cor24-examples
106	
107	cor24-tutorials
108	
109	Repo strategy: monorepo vs many repos
110	For your case, I would not unify everything into one implementation repo.
111	
112	Use:
113	
114	One hub repo: cor24-project
115	
116	Many focused implementation repos
117	
118	Why that is the better fit:
119	
120	Pros
121	Each subsystem has a clean identity.
122	
123	Easier to explain maturity per project.
124	
125	Easier to open-source incrementally.
126	
127	Easier to keep Rust/C/Pascal/Forth work separated.
128	
129	Cons
130	Cross-repo issues and roadmap are harder.
131	
132	Shared docs can duplicate.

134	Alternative
135	If some projects are tiny or tightly coupled, combine them selectively, for example:
136

138	
139	compiler

143	linker
144	
145	p-code assembler
146	
147	p-code VM
148	
149	That would make sense if those pieces evolve together and are mostly consumed together.
150

152	I would standardize hard now. Something like this:
153

155	cor24-project
156	
157	cor24-spec
158	
159	cor24-rs

161	cor24-c
162

file.content:
I have a bunch of projects under: github.com/{softwarewrighter,sw-embed,sw-vibe-coding} that are related to the COR24 ISA (makerlisp.com).  I want to create a new organization and repo to unify this, maybe sw-cor24.  I want a repo cor24-project, that unifies (lists, links-to, explains) the projects and there relationshiip.  e.g. cor24-rs has an assembler and emulator for the COR24-TD board.  there is a C compiler, a Macro Lisp system, a FORTH interpreter, a p-code VM, a p-code assembler, a Pascal compiler, a Pascal runtime, a Pascal linker, and web demos for the cor24-rs assembler/emulator, the C compiler, the Macro Lisp, the Forth, the p-code VM and the Pascal compiler (and soon, a BASIC built on p-code, maybe an APL, too)


Thought for 10s

Received app response

Thought
You want a top-level GitHub organization and “portal” repo that makes the COR24 ecosystem legible: what exists, what depends on what, what runs where, what is stable vs experimental, and where the web demos live.

High-level recommendation
I would do this:

Organization: sw-cor24

Top-level hub repo: cor24-project

Keep most existing implementation repos separate rather than merging them into one giant monorepo.

Use cor24-project as the map, not the implementation.

Add one org profile README and pin the 6 most important repos so the org page itself becomes a landing page. GitHub organizations are designed as shared containers for related repositories, and GitHub supports both an organization profile README and pinned repositories on the org overview page. 

That gives you:

Benefits
Clear story for newcomers.

No forced repo migration risk before the structure is proven.

Independent release cadence for assembler/emulator vs C compiler vs Pascal toolchain vs demos.

A place to explain architecture across Rust, C, Lisp, Forth, Pascal, p-code, and web demos.

Risks
Too many small repos can still feel fragmented.

Links can rot unless you maintain the hub repo.

Naming drift will confuse people unless you impose conventions now.

Recommended architecture
Think in layers.

1. Hardware / board / ISA layer
This explains what COR24 is and what board variants exist.

Repos here might include:

cor24-spec

cor24-boards

cor24-fpga or external MakerLisp references

cor24-project links to MakerLisp COR24 overview

MakerLisp’s COR24 material already gives you a natural anchor for the ISA/soft-CPU story. 

2. Core tooling layer
These are the foundation repos.

Examples:

cor24-rs — assembler + emulator for COR24-TD

cor24-c or cor24-cc — C compiler

cor24-web or separate demo repos if needed

3. Runtime / language layer
These sit on top of the core tooling.

Examples:

cor24-macrolisp

cor24-forth

cor24-pcode-vm

cor24-pcode-asm

cor24-pascal

later:

cor24-basic

cor24-apl

4. Demo / teaching / docs layer
These are outward-facing and easier for people to try.

Examples:

cor24-demos

cor24-web-demos

cor24-examples

cor24-tutorials

Repo strategy: monorepo vs many repos
For your case, I would not unify everything into one implementation repo.

Use:

One hub repo: cor24-project

Many focused implementation repos

Why that is the better fit:

Pros
Each subsystem has a clean identity.

Easier to explain maturity per project.

Easier to open-source incrementally.

Easier to keep Rust/C/Pascal/Forth work separated.

Cons
Cross-repo issues and roadmap are harder.

Shared docs can duplicate.

Alternative
If some projects are tiny or tightly coupled, combine them selectively, for example:

cor24-pascal-suite

compiler

runtime

linker

p-code assembler

p-code VM

That would make sense if those pieces evolve together and are mostly consumed together.

Naming convention
I would standardize hard now. Something like this:

Core
cor24-project

cor24-spec

cor24-rs

cor24-c

Languages
cor24-macrolisp

cor24-forth

cor24-pcode-vm

cor24-pcode-asm

cor24-pascal

cor24-basic

cor24-apl

Web / demos / docs
cor24-web-demos

cor24-examples

cor24-docs

Avoid mixing prefixes like sw-, cor24-, makerlisp-, and random historical names unless there is a strong reason. The hub repo should document old names and the new canonical names.

What cor24-project should contain
The hub repo should answer these questions in under 60 seconds:

What is COR24?

What can I run today?

What are the major subsystems?

In what order should I learn them?

Which repos are active, experimental, or archived?

How do the components relate?

How do the components relate?

I would structure it like this:

cor24-project/
  README.md
  docs/
    overview.md
    architecture.md
    roadmap.md
    getting-started.md
    project-status.md
    repo-index.md
    demos.md
    language-stack.md
    board-targets.md
  diagrams/
    ecosystem-overview.svg
    toolchain-flow.svg
    dependency-graph.svg
  examples/
    hello-world-links.md
  .github/
    ISSUE_TEMPLATE/
    DISCUSSION_TEMPLATE/
Suggested README outline for cor24-project
Top section
2–3 sentence description

one architecture image

quick links:

assembler/emulator

C compiler

Macro Lisp

Forth

p-code VM

Pascal

demos

Then these sections
1. What is COR24?
Brief explanation of:

COR24 ISA

COR24-TD board

emulator and FPGA/board relationship

MakerLisp relationship

2. Ecosystem map
A table like:

Project	Purpose	Language	Status	Depends on	Demo
cor24-rs	Assembler + emulator	Rust	active	none	yes
cor24-c	C compiler	C/Rust/etc	active	assembler/emulator	yes
cor24-macrolisp	Macro Lisp system	C + Lisp	active	cor24-c / runtime	yes
cor24-forth	Forth interpreter	asm/Forth	active	assembler/emulator	yes
cor24-pcode-vm	p-code VM	asm/C	active	assembler/emulator	yes
cor24-pcode-asm	p-code assembler	Rust/C	active	p-code VM	maybe
cor24-pascal	Pascal compiler	C/Pascal	active	p-code tools	yes
3. Dependency flow
Something like:

COR24 ISA / Board
   ↓
Assembler + Emulator
   ↓
C compiler
   ↓
Macro Lisp / Forth / p-code VM
   ↓
p-code assembler
   ↓
Pascal compiler + runtime + linker
   ↓
Future BASIC / APL / web demos
4. Learning paths
Different readers need different entry points:

I want to try something in the browser

I want to understand the ISA

I want to write COR24 assembly

I want to experiment with Pascal and p-code

I want to study bootstrapping of languages on custom hardware

That is one of the highest-value sections.

Recommended diagram set
You should include at least these 3 diagrams.

1. Ecosystem overview
Shows repo boxes and arrows.

2. Toolchain flow
Example:

Source (ASM / C / Lisp / Forth / Pascal)
   ↓
Compiler / Assembler / Translator
   ↓
COR24 binaries or p-code
   ↓
Emulator / VM / Web demo / Hardware
3. Historical / conceptual stack
Example:

Hardware / ISA
Tooling
Runtimes
Languages
Demos / Education
Org-level setup
At the GitHub organization level, I would do:

org name: sw-cor24

public org profile README in .github/profile/README.md

pin these six repos first:

cor24-project

cor24-rs

cor24-c

cor24-macrolisp

cor24-forth

cor24-pascal

GitHub allows an organization overview README and pinned repositories, which makes this a very good fit for your “unifying front door” goal. 

Migration plan
Do this in phases.

Phase 1: create the umbrella without breaking anything
create org sw-cor24

create cor24-project

add org profile README

pin key repos

write the ecosystem map

link to existing repos in softwarewrighter, sw-embed, sw-vibe-coding

Phase 2: normalize names
decide canonical names

rename only the most visible repos first

leave redirects in place

add “formerly known as” notes in README

Phase 3: move repos selectively
Move only repos that clearly belong in the new org and are stable enough to keep public-facing.

Phase 4: unify issues/docs if desired
Later, if needed:

use org discussions

centralize roadmap/issues in cor24-project

leave implementation-specific issues in implementation repos

How I would describe the relationships
Use both technical relationships and conceptual relationships.

Technical
“depends on”

“targets”

“runs on”

“emits p-code for”

“consumes output of”

Conceptual

409	
410	That matters because some projects are not just dependencies; they are part of the learning story.
411	
412	Example wording for the main description
413	You could say something like:
414	
415	The COR24 project is a family of tools, language implementations, runtimes, and demos built around the MakerLisp COR24 ISA and related boards. It includes the core assembler and emulator, higher-level language tooling such as a C compiler, Macro Lisp, Forth, and Pascal, a p-code toolchain, and browser-based demos that make the system easier to explore and teach.
416	
417	That is clear and future-proof.
418	
419	One important design choice
420	I would make cor24-project primarily a documentation and navigation repo, not a code repo.
421	
422	That keeps it stable and makes it easier to maintain.
423	
424	If you later want one place that checks ecosystem health, you can add:
425	
426	link validation
427	
428	generated repo status tables
429	
430	badges
431	
432	a script that builds the dependency graph from YAML/JSON metadata
433	
434	Recommended metadata file
435	In cor24-project, keep a machine-readable catalog like:
436	
437	projects:
438	  - name: cor24-rs
439	    category: core-tooling
440	    status: active
441	    language: Rust
442	    purpose: assembler and emulator for COR24-TD
443	    depends_on: []
444	    outputs: [assembled binaries, emulator runs]
445	    demo: true
446	
447	  - name: cor24-pascal
448	    category: language
449	    status: active
450	    language: C, Pascal
451	    purpose: Pascal compiler targeting p-code
452	    depends_on: [cor24-pcode-vm, cor24-pcode-asm]
453	    demo: true
454	Then you can generate tables and diagrams from it.
455	
456	My opinion on the org/repo names
457	Organization
458	sw-cor24 is good.
459	
460	cor24 would be even better if available.
461	
462	cor24-projects is okay but less elegant.
463	
464	makerlisp-cor24 is accurate but may imply official MakerLisp ownership if that is not the intent.
465	
466	Hub repo
467	cor24-project is good.
468	
469	cor24-overview is clearer but less broad.
470	
471	awesome-cor24 sounds community-curated; probably not what you want.
472	
473	cor24-ecosystem is also strong.
474	
475	My preference:
476	
477	Org: sw-cor24
478	
479	Hub repo: cor24-project
480	
481	What I would do next
482	Create sw-cor24.
483	
484	Create cor24-project.
485	
486	Add:
487	
488	README.md
489	
490	docs/repo-index.md
491	
492	docs/architecture.md
493	
494	docs/getting-started.md
495	
496	Add one SVG diagram.
497	
498	Link existing repos first; move repos later.
499	
500	Standardize naming only after the documentation reveals the clean grouping.
501	
502	One note: I tried to inspect your GitHub repos through the GitHub connector, but no repositories are currently selected there, so I could not verify the exact current repo inventory from your account. I therefore based the structure above on your description plus GitHub’s current organization/profile capabilities and MakerLisp’s public COR24 page. 
503	
504	I can draft the initial cor24-project/README.md and a repo-index.md next.
505	
506	
507	one thing of importance: cor24 is not my branding, it is makerlisp.com's.  My branding is softwarewrighter and sw-xxx.  Also many projects have Tiny in their names (I do not claim these are complete/usable/supported tools, instead these are educational examples of vibe-coding and personal-software, part of a grander vision using FPGAs to build clusters running my own sw-os (operating system inspired by MenuetOS and OS/390).  so, cor24-c means (to some people) makerlisp's own C compiler not mine.  Also makerlisp has a scheme for cor24, mine is less-scheme-like alternative.  so sw-cor24-xxx is better naming, and note the Tiny for C, and macrolisp.  I did not use Tiny for FORTH as it is/or could be a full FORTH.  The Pascal could be renamed Tiny, but it is meant to be a "full" Turbo-Pascal inspired code (for embedded).    Motivation: (1) improve my vibe-coding foo and tools, (2) have a complete dev tool chain that runs on FPGA hardware, (3) run that tool chain in a browser for educational, blogging/vlogging use.
508	
509	That changes the architecture in an important way.
510	
511	Restated requirements
512	You do not want to imply ownership of the COR24 brand, ISA, or MakerLisp ecosystem. You want:
513	
514	your own namespace and branding: softwarewrighter / sw-*
515	
516	a hub that explains your projects for COR24, without sounding official
517	
518	naming that signals:
519	
520	educational
521	
522	experimental
523	
524	personal-software / vibe-coding
525	
526	not necessarily production-grade
527	
528	room for two classes of language tooling:
529	
530	Tiny tools for learning, bootstrapping, and demos
531	
532	more ambitious / fuller tools like Forth and Turbo-Pascal-inspired Pascal
533	
534	a larger framing around:
535	
536	improving your vibe-coding process and tools
537	
538	building a complete dev toolchain that can run on FPGA-hosted systems
539	
540	running the toolchain in a browser for education, blog posts, and videos
541	
542	That means the repo architecture should be explicitly “Softwarewrighter projects targeting COR24”, not “the COR24 project.”
543	
544	Revised branding strategy
545	Core principle
546	Use:
547	
548	sw-... for your work
549	
550	...-cor24-... only as a target descriptor
551	
552	avoid bare cor24-* names because they can sound canonical or official

file.content:
“teaching/demo”

“bootstrap path”

“experimental language”

“runtime/tooling”

“browser demo”

That matters because some projects are not just dependencies; they are part of the learning story.

Example wording for the main description
You could say something like:

The COR24 project is a family of tools, language implementations, runtimes, and demos built around the MakerLisp COR24 ISA and related boards. It includes the core assembler and emulator, higher-level language tooling such as a C compiler, Macro Lisp, Forth, and Pascal, a p-code toolchain, and browser-based demos that make the system easier to explore and teach.

That is clear and future-proof.

One important design choice
I would make cor24-project primarily a documentation and navigation repo, not a code repo.

That keeps it stable and makes it easier to maintain.

If you later want one place that checks ecosystem health, you can add:

link validation

generated repo status tables

badges

a script that builds the dependency graph from YAML/JSON metadata

Recommended metadata file
In cor24-project, keep a machine-readable catalog like:

projects:
  - name: cor24-rs
    category: core-tooling
    status: active
    language: Rust
    purpose: assembler and emulator for COR24-TD
    depends_on: []
    outputs: [assembled binaries, emulator runs]
    demo: true

  - name: cor24-pascal
    category: language
    status: active
    language: C, Pascal
    purpose: Pascal compiler targeting p-code
    depends_on: [cor24-pcode-vm, cor24-pcode-asm]
    demo: true
Then you can generate tables and diagrams from it.

My opinion on the org/repo names
Organization
sw-cor24 is good.

cor24 would be even better if available.

cor24-projects is okay but less elegant.

makerlisp-cor24 is accurate but may imply official MakerLisp ownership if that is not the intent.

Hub repo
cor24-project is good.

cor24-overview is clearer but less broad.

awesome-cor24 sounds community-curated; probably not what you want.

cor24-ecosystem is also strong.

My preference:

Org: sw-cor24

Hub repo: cor24-project

What I would do next
Create sw-cor24.

Create cor24-project.

Add:

README.md

docs/repo-index.md

docs/architecture.md

docs/getting-started.md

Add one SVG diagram.

Link existing repos first; move repos later.

Standardize naming only after the documentation reveals the clean grouping.

One note: I tried to inspect your GitHub repos through the GitHub connector, but no repositories are currently selected there, so I could not verify the exact current repo inventory from your account. I therefore based the structure above on your description plus GitHub’s current organization/profile capabilities and MakerLisp’s public COR24 page. 

I can draft the initial cor24-project/README.md and a repo-index.md next.


one thing of importance: cor24 is not my branding, it is makerlisp.com's.  My branding is softwarewrighter and sw-xxx.  Also many projects have Tiny in their names (I do not claim these are complete/usable/supported tools, instead these are educational examples of vibe-coding and personal-software, part of a grander vision using FPGAs to build clusters running my own sw-os (operating system inspired by MenuetOS and OS/390).  so, cor24-c means (to some people) makerlisp's own C compiler not mine.  Also makerlisp has a scheme for cor24, mine is less-scheme-like alternative.  so sw-cor24-xxx is better naming, and note the Tiny for C, and macrolisp.  I did not use Tiny for FORTH as it is/or could be a full FORTH.  The Pascal could be renamed Tiny, but it is meant to be a "full" Turbo-Pascal inspired code (for embedded).    Motivation: (1) improve my vibe-coding foo and tools, (2) have a complete dev tool chain that runs on FPGA hardware, (3) run that tool chain in a browser for educational, blogging/vlogging use.

That changes the architecture in an important way.

Restated requirements
You do not want to imply ownership of the COR24 brand, ISA, or MakerLisp ecosystem. You want:

your own namespace and branding: softwarewrighter / sw-*

a hub that explains your projects for COR24, without sounding official

naming that signals:

educational

experimental

personal-software / vibe-coding

not necessarily production-grade

room for two classes of language tooling:

Tiny tools for learning, bootstrapping, and demos

more ambitious / fuller tools like Forth and Turbo-Pascal-inspired Pascal

a larger framing around:

improving your vibe-coding process and tools

building a complete dev toolchain that can run on FPGA-hosted systems

running the toolchain in a browser for education, blog posts, and videos

That means the repo architecture should be explicitly “Softwarewrighter projects targeting COR24”, not “the COR24 project.”

Revised branding strategy
Core principle
Use:

sw-... for your work

...-cor24-... only as a target descriptor

avoid bare cor24-* names because they can sound canonical or official

So instead of:

cor24-c

cor24-project

cor24-pascal

prefer:

sw-cor24-project

sw-tiny-c-cor24

sw-tiny-macrolisp-cor24

sw-forth-cor24

sw-pascal-cor24

This makes authorship and scope obvious.

High-level repo/org recommendation
Organization
I would now strongly favor:

GitHub org: softwarewrighter

if you want one org for your whole ecosystem

or GitHub org: sw-vibe-coding

if you want the org itself to emphasize the experimental/vibe-coded angle

or GitHub org: sw-cor24-lab

if you want a dedicated sub-brand for this family

My recommendation
If your long-term vision extends beyond COR24 into your own OS, FPGA clusters, browser-hosted toolchains, and educational systems work, then the cleanest structure is:

Org: softwarewrighter

Hub repo: sw-cor24-project

Why:

If softwarewrighter as an org is not practical, then:

is also very good.

Naming architecture
You now have at least three semantic axes in play:

1. Ownership / brand
Your work should clearly say sw-.

2. Target platform
These tools target COR24, so cor24 should stay in the name.

3. Maturity / intent
You want to distinguish:

tiny / educational / exploratory

fuller / serious / growing systems

That suggests a predictable naming grammar.

Recommended grammar
Pattern A: educational/experimental tools
sw-tiny-<tool>-cor24

Examples:

sw-tiny-c-cor24

sw-tiny-macrolisp-cor24

sw-tiny-basic-cor24

sw-tiny-pcode-asm-cor24

Pattern B: fuller tools
sw-<tool>-cor24

Examples:

sw-forth-cor24

sw-pascal-cor24

sw-pcode-vm-cor24

sw-linker-cor24 or sw-pascal-linker-cor24

Pattern C: umbrella / docs / demos
sw-cor24-<thing>

Examples:

sw-cor24-project

sw-cor24-demos

sw-cor24-web

sw-cor24-examples

sw-cor24-docs

This is much cleaner than putting cor24 first everywhere.

How “Tiny” should be used
Your instinct is good: “Tiny” is not just about missing features; it signals:

educational

intentionally scoped

bootstrapping-oriented

personal-software

good for vibe-coding and explanation

not making grand claims

That is very valuable branding.

But it should be used selectively.

Good candidates for Tiny
C

Macro Lisp

BASIC

p-code assembler

perhaps the first Pascal compiler stage if it is truly subset-oriented

Not ideal for Tiny
Forth, if the goal is a reasonably authentic and extensible Forth

Pascal, if the long-term intent is “small but real Turbo-Pascal-inspired embedded Pascal”

p-code VM, if it is a core runtime substrate rather than a toy

So I would keep:

sw-forth-cor24

sw-pcode-vm-cor24

sw-pascal-cor24

rather than trying to force Tiny onto everything.

Revised repo set
Here is a naming set that matches your stated goals.

Hub / overview
sw-cor24-project
The portal repo: lists, links, explains, diagrams, motivations, roadmap.

Core tooling
sw-cor24-rs
Rust assembler + emulator + maybe core utilities.

sw-tiny-c-cor24
Your educational C compiler targeting COR24.

Language systems
sw-tiny-macrolisp-cor24
Your less-Scheme-like Lisp alternative.

sw-forth-cor24

sw-pcode-vm-cor24

future:

sw-apl-cor24 or sw-tiny-apl-cor24 depending scope

Browser / demos / education
sw-cor24-web-demos

or, if you want demo code colocated with each tool, keep the demos in each repo and let sw-cor24-project link to them

What the hub repo should now say
The hub repo should not present itself as “the COR24 project.”

It should present itself as something like:

Softwarewrighter’s collection of educational, experimental, and evolving toolchain projects targeting the MakerLisp COR24 ISA and boards.

That phrasing does four things well:

preserves MakerLisp ownership/credit

makes your target platform explicit

frames the projects as yours

avoids overclaiming completeness

Recommended top-level positioning statement
A good opening paragraph for sw-cor24-project would be along these lines:

sw-cor24-project is a guide to Softwarewrighter’s educational and experimental tooling for the MakerLisp COR24 ISA. These projects explore bootstrapping language tooling, browser-based demos, FPGA-hosted development systems, and personal vibe-coding workflows. Some tools are intentionally “Tiny” to keep them learnable and explainable; others aim toward fuller embedded systems such as Forth and a Turbo-Pascal-inspired Pascal environment.

That expresses the philosophy, not just the inventory.

The three motivations should be first-class structure
These are not side notes. They should drive the hub repo.

I would make them a top-level section
Why this exists
1. Vibe-coding and personal software
These repos are a laboratory for improving your own software creation process, including AI-assisted design, incremental tool-building, and educationally useful implementations.

This turns the repo from a pile of links into a coherent research/development program.

browser-demos.md supports your vlogging/blogging angle

Project relationship model
You said the hub must unify and explain relationships. I would classify relationships in four ways.

1. Target relationship
“targets COR24”

“runs on emulator / FPGA board / browser demo”

2. Build relationship
“produces assembly”

“produces p-code”

3. Bootstrap relationship

4. Intent relationship
tiny / educational

That gives you a much better README table than a flat list.

Suggested README table shape
Repo	Role	Intent	Output	Runs where	Notes
sw-cor24-rs	assembler + emulator	core substrate	COR24 binaries / emulator runs	native, web demos	foundation repo
sw-tiny-c-cor24	small C compiler	educational / bootstrap	COR24 asm or binaries	native, maybe web	not official MakerLisp C
sw-tiny-macrolisp-cor24	Lisp system	educational / alternative Lisp	interpreted / compiled forms	native, web	intentionally less Scheme-like

This already tells a story.

Repo boundaries
You also need to decide whether Pascal pieces stay together or split.

My recommendation
For Pascal, I would likely use one umbrella repo at first:

and inside it:

they will evolve together

Option B: combine
sw-pcode-cor24

with subdirs:

vm/

asm/

docs/

I slightly prefer Option B unless the VM and assembler are already independently mature.

Here is the set I would most seriously consider.

Preferred set

Tiny means small, explainable, and educational

not necessarily trivial

not necessarily disposable

Risk 3: COR24 still dominates the name visually
Even with sw-, some people may still scan and assume official status.

Mitigation
Put this near the top of the README:

These are independent Softwarewrighter projects targeting the MakerLisp COR24 platform. They are not the official MakerLisp tools.

That one sentence prevents a lot of confusion.

Best positioning sentence for the README
I would include something like this very early:

This repository maps Softwarewrighter’s independent COR24-targeting projects: small educational compilers and language systems, fuller experimental environments such as Forth and Pascal, and browser-hosted demos used for teaching, blogging, and video creation.

That is very close to your intent.

Concrete recommendation
If you want one clean answer, mine is:

Use this scheme
Org
softwarewrighter if available and aligned with your larger vision
otherwise

sw-cor24-lab

Hub repo
sw-cor24-project

Main repos
sw-cor24-rs

sw-tiny-c-cor24

sw-tiny-macrolisp-cor24

sw-forth-cor24

sw-pcode-cor24

sw-pascal-cor24

sw-cor24-web-demos

What I would do next
I would next draft these three things in order:

1. A short naming manifesto
A doc explaining:

why sw-

why cor24 appears only as target

what “Tiny” means

how repo names are formed

2. The top-level sw-cor24-project/README.md
Including:

disclaimer about MakerLisp ownership

motivations

repo map

relationships

learning paths

3. A project metadata table
A YAML or markdown table that you can maintain as the ecosystem evolves

The next useful artifact is the actual first-pass README.md for sw-cor24-project.


Some other naming considerations.  To keep clear in my head (and potentially help others) I have been using a suffix to denote the main implementation language of a project, hence cor24-rs is written in Rust.  When I started, it combined the assembler and emulator into one command (one use-case: run this assembler).  It has evloved and I want to split it into two repos (or two components of a repo).  The components are cor24-assembler-rs and cor24-emulator-rs.  The forth compiler is named tf24a (Tiny Forth for COR24 in assembler) - so we need to rename to sw-cor24-forth-a.  The p24p/p24c is confusing. repo is p24c, s/b sw-cor24-pascal-c. it was developed in p24p because of a mistake (p is for p-code--it runs in p-code but is compiled from C).  Anway I need to have phases: (1) add umbrella org, repo, readme, live demo w/links to live demos--soon; (2) start renaming repos, (2) combine all the pascal-p-code related ones, but it is more like (a) p-code tools and VM, (b) Pascal compiler and tools, (c) BASIC compiler/tools.  So many repos down to 3; (3) move repos under umbrella org (4) ensure all dependencies, builds, tests, links, documentation reflect the final naming, locations, tools, demos

You are defining a stable naming system, migration path, and repo consolidation plan for a growing COR24-targeting ecosystem under your own sw-* brand, while preserving a strong signal for:

implementation language via suffixes like -rs, -c, -a

target platform via cor24

tool role via names like assembler, emulator, pascal, pcode, forth

authorship/branding via sw-

future consolidation without losing conceptual clarity

That is the right time to impose structure.

High-level recommendation
I think your current instincts are solid, and I would formalize them into a naming grammar plus a phased migration.

Recommended naming grammar
Use this pattern:

sw-cor24-<tool-or-domain>-<impl>

Where:

sw = your brand

cor24 = target platform

<tool-or-domain> = assembler, emulator, forth, pascal, pcode, basic, etc.

<impl> = main implementation language

rs = Rust

c = C

a = assembler

optionally later:

fth

lisp

pas

but I would avoid too many suffix styles unless truly needed

This gives you names that are machine-sortable, human-readable, and mentally consistent.

Naming architecture
1. Core tools
You want to split current cor24-rs into two distinct components. That makes sense.

Recommended names
sw-cor24-assembler-rs

sw-cor24-emulator-rs

Why this is good
each repo/component does one thing

implementation language remains obvious

the target remains explicit

it avoids the ambiguity of cor24-rs, which could mean “the Rust COR24 project”

Tradeoff
The names are longer.

Why I still prefer them
Because clarity beats shortness here. You are building a family, not a single throwaway repo.

2. Forth
You have historical name tf24a = Tiny Forth for COR24 in assembler.

You now want something under the unified naming scheme.

Your proposal
sw-cor24-forth-a

I think that is good.

My slight refinement
Use either:

sw-cor24-forth-a if you want the shortest practical name
or

sw-cor24-forth-asm if you want the suffix to be more obvious to casual readers

My preference
-a is elegant and consistent if you already use it in your head

-asm is more self-explanatory to outsiders

Because you care about your own internal clarity and a consistent family, I think sw-cor24-forth-a is acceptable and probably best.

Important point
I would not force Tiny into the new public name unless you want the philosophy foregrounded. Since you said this can become a fuller Forth, sw-cor24-forth-a is better than sw-tiny-forth-cor24-a.

3. Pascal
You said the current repo is p24c, but it was developed in p24p by mistake because the “p” referred to p-code rather than Pascal source language.

That confusion is exactly what your new naming scheme should eliminate.

Recommended Pascal compiler name
sw-cor24-pascal-c

That is strong.

It tells you:

1700	You are really deciding what the names are for.
1701	
1702	That is the root of the confusion.
1703	
1704	Restated design problem
1705	You currently have several overlapping dimensions:
1706	
1707	target: COR24
1708	
1709	brand: sw-
1710	
1711	domain/tool role: assembler, emulator, macrolisp, forth, pcode, pascal, basic
1712	
1713	implementation language: Rust, C, assembler, Pascal
1714	
1715	execution substrate: native host, COR24, p-code VM, browser
1716	
1717	strategic intent: educational, experimental, fuller system, bootstrap tool
1718	
1719	If repo names try to encode too many of those at once, they become hard to reason about and easy to misread.
1720	
1721	So the main architectural question is:
1722	
1723	What belongs in the repo name, and what belongs in documentation?
1724	My recommendation now is:
1725	
1726	Drop implementation-language suffixes from most repo names
1727	I think you are right to reconsider them.
1728	
1729	Use repo names for:
1730	
1731	brand
1732	
1733	target
1734	
1735	concept / role
1736	
1737	Put implementation language, execution mode, maturity, and bootstrapping notes into a canonical table in the umbrella repo.
1738	
1739	That is the cleanest model.
1740	
1741	Why suffixes are hurting more than helping
1742	At first, suffixes like -rs, -c, -a seem useful because they provide quick memory cues.
1743	
1744	But in your ecosystem they break down because the reality is messier:
1745	
1746	one project may contain multiple languages
1747	
1748	a project may be implemented in C but target p-code
1749	
1750	Pascal runtime pieces may be written partly in Pascal and partly in p-code assembler
1751	
1752	something may start in Rust and later move partly to C or assembler
1753	
1754	“where it runs” is often more important than “what it is written in”
1755	
1756	p-code is not an implementation language, it is a VM instruction layer / intermediate representation / execution substrate
1757	
1758	So a suffix gives a false sense of precision.
1759	
1760	Example
1761	sw-cor24-pascal-c
1762	
1763	What does that actually tell you?
1764	
1765	Pascal compiler written in C?
1766	
1767	Pascal system with a C runtime?
1768	
1769	Pascal compiler for COR24 that emits C?
1770	
1771	repository mostly C today, but later mixed?
1772	
1773	It helps a little, but it also misleads.
1774	
1775	The deeper conceptual distinction
1776	You are mixing up at least three different kinds of “language”:
1777	
1778	1. Implementation language
1779	What the repo is written in:
1780	
1781	Rust
1782	
1783	C
1784	
1785	assembler
1786	
1787	Pascal
1788	
1789	2. Source language
1790	What the tool processes:
1791	
1792	Pascal compiler processes Pascal
1793	
1794	Forth interpreter processes Forth
1795	
1796	Lisp system processes Lisp
1797	
1798	3. Target / execution form
1799	What it emits or runs on:
1800	
1801	COR24 machine code
1802	
1803	p-code
1804	
1805	browser demo
1806	
1807	FPGA-hosted runtime
1808	
1809	These are separate columns, not something you want compressed into a suffix.
1810	
1811	Recommendation: use semantic repo names, not implementation names
1812	Preferred repo-name pattern
1813	Use:
1814	
1815	sw-cor24-<domain>
1816	
1817	Examples:
1818	
1819	sw-cor24-project
1820	
1821	sw-cor24-assembler
1822	
1823	sw-cor24-emulator
1824	
1825	sw-cor24-macrolisp
1826	
1827	sw-cor24-forth
1828	
1829	sw-cor24-pcode
1830	
1831	sw-cor24-pascal
1832	
1833	sw-cor24-basic
1834	
1835	sw-cor24-apl
1836	
1837	sw-cor24-web-demos
1838	
1839	That is much easier to think about.
1840	
1841	These names answer the most important public question:
1842	
1843	What is this repo about?
1844	
1845	They do not try to answer:
1846	
1847	what language is it written in
1848	
1849	whether it runs on FPGA yet
1850	
1851	whether it emits p-code or machine code
1852	
1853	whether it is tiny/full/experimental
1854	
1855	whether it is bootstrapping or self-hosting yet
1856	
1857	Those belong in documentation.

1859	What should replace the suffixes?
1860	A good authoritative table in sw-cor24-project.
1861	
1862	I think your intuition there is exactly right.

1864	The hub repo becomes your memory system
1865	Instead of encoding every nuance in names, create a table like this:
1866	
1867	Repo	Purpose	Source language / user model	Implemented in	Emits / runs on	Runs where today	Long-term direction
1868	sw-cor24-assembler	assemble COR24 source	COR24 asm	Rust	COR24 binaries	host, browser demo	maybe more self-hosting later
1869	sw-cor24-emulator	emulate COR24 board/system	n/a	Rust	executes COR24 binaries	host, browser demo	FPGA-adjacent teaching/demo tool
1870	sw-cor24-macrolisp	macro-lisp system	Lisp	C + asm + Lisp	COR24/native forms	host now, COR24 later	basis for OS/tooling
1871	sw-cor24-forth	Forth system	Forth	assembler	direct/native behavior	emulator/hardware	fuller Forth
1872	sw-cor24-pcode	p-code VM + tools	p-code / VM substrate	mixed	executes p-code	host, emulator, browser	substrate for Pascal/BASIC
1873	sw-cor24-pascal	Pascal compiler/toolchain	Pascal	C + Pascal + p-code parts	p-code	host now	fuller embedded Pascal
1874	sw-cor24-basic	BASIC compiler/toolchain	BASIC	TBD	p-code	planned	teaching + embedded use

1877	How to think about p-code
1878	This is one of the main sources of confusion, so it helps to make it explicit.
1879	
1880	p-code should not usually be treated like a suffix category
1881	Because p-code is not primarily:
1882	
1883	a source language
1884	
1885	an implementation language
1886	
1887	a branding axis
1888	
1889	It is more like:
1890	
1891	a VM instruction set
1892	
1893	an intermediate representation
1894	
1895	a runtime substrate
1896	
1897	a testing/bootstrapping layer
1898	
1899	So I would not use -p or -pcode as a suffix meaning “written in p-code,” unless you truly have a repo whose primary identity is “this is a p-code program.”
1900

1902	
1903	sw-cor24-pcode is the subsystem repo

1906	
1907	VM
1908	
1909	p-code assembler
1910	
1911	maybe test programs
1912	
1913	low-level runtime pieces

1915	That is enough.

1917	You do not need suffixes like:
1918	
1919	-p
1920	
1921	-pcode
1922	
1923	-vmcode
1924	
1925	etc.
1926	
1927	except when pcode is the actual repo subject.

2352	Why separate org-per-ISA is probably the wrong first move
2353	You proposed something like:
2354	
2355	sw-cor24
2356	
2357	sw-s1130
2358	
2359	sw-s370
2360	
2361	etc.
2362	
2363	That can work, but I think it is too fragmented for where you are now.
2364	
2365	Pros
2366	very clean conceptual isolation
2367	
2368	easy mental separation by ISA family
2369	
2370	each org can look self-contained
2371	
2372	Cons
2373	too many orgs too early
2374	
2375	repeated profile README/admin/config overhead
2376	
2377	harder cross-family discoverability
2378	
2379	harder to show the shared grand vision
2380	
2381	more painful if projects span families
2382	
2383	Since you already know these families are conceptually related, a single software umbrella org is probably better.
2384	
2385	My recommendation
2386	Use repos for ISA partitioning, not orgs, at least initially.

2388	So:
2389	
2390	one software umbrella org: sw-embed
2391	
2392	one hardware umbrella org: hardwarewrighter or hw-embed
2393	
2394	Then inside each, partition by repo names.

2994	Important GitHub nuance
2995	A GitHub organization is not actually nested under a personal account or another organization in the ownership sense. GitHub’s account types are separate: personal accounts, organization accounts, and enterprise accounts. Organizations are shared accounts; people sign in with personal accounts and can own or administer organizations. If you want true central management of multiple organizations, that is the role of a GitHub enterprise account, not one org “inside” another. 
2996	
2997	So the practical interpretation of your idea is:
2998	
2999	softwarewrighter is your main identity / top-level brand
3000	
3001	hardwarewrighter is a separate org you control
3002	
3003	both can coexist without introducing another unrelated top-level brand
3004	
3005	you manage them from your personal/admin access, rather than by literally nesting one under the other. 
3006	
3007	Why this is a good fit
3008	This gives you:
3009	
3010	one clear company/owner identity: softwarewrighter
3011	
3012	one clear software umbrella: sw-embed
3013	
3014	one clear hardware umbrella: hardwarewrighter
3015	
3016	That matches your actual architecture:
3017	
3018	software ecosystems span multiple ISA families
3019	
3020	hardware ecosystems span multiple FPGA soft CPUs and board ports
3021	
3022	COR24 becomes just one family among several, rather than the top-level container
3023	
3024	My strongest recommendation
3025	I would treat the hierarchy like this conceptually:
3026	
3027	Softwarewrighter (company / owner / main brand)
3028	├── sw-embed          # software org
3029	│   ├── sw-embed-project
3030	│   ├── sw-cor24-project
3031	│   ├── sw-s1130-project
3032	│   └── ...
3033	└── hardwarewrighter  # hardware org
3034	    ├── hw-cor24
3035	    ├── hw-s1130
3036	    └── ...
3037	Even though GitHub won’t model that as literal nested ownership, it works very well as a branding and portfolio structure. GitHub organizations are intended as branded containers for shared work, and enterprise accounts are the layer above organizations if you ever need centralized org governance later. 
3038	
3039	Suggested next move
3040	Start with just these three anchors:
3041	
3042	softwarewrighter as the public parent brand
3043	
3044	sw-embed for software repos
3045	
3046	hardwarewrighter for hardware repos
3047	
3048	Then create:
3049	
3050	sw-embed-project
3051	
3052	sw-cor24-project
3053	
3054	hw-cor24
3055	
3056	That gives you a stable structure without overcommitting to too many orgs or names at once.

file.content:
branded by you

targets COR24

is Pascal-related

main implementation is C

Why it works better than p24c
p24c is too compressed and ambiguous

newcomers will not know whether p means p-code, Pascal, project, or platform

sw-cor24-pascal-c makes the source-language/domain explicit

Consolidation architecture
You want to consolidate many Pascal/p-code-related repos down to about three conceptual groups:

(a) p-code tools and VM

(b) Pascal compiler and tools

(c) BASIC compiler/tools

That is a very good consolidation boundary.

Recommended final grouping
A. p-code substrate
This is the runtime/tool substrate.

Suggested repo:

sw-cor24-pcode-c if mostly C

or sw-cor24-pcode-rs if Rust

or, if mixed and intended as a domain umbrella:

sw-cor24-pcode

My recommendation
If the implementation language matters strongly in your mental model, use the suffix.
If the repo is going to contain mixed-language pieces and act as a conceptual umbrella, omit the suffix.

For this one, I slightly prefer:

sw-cor24-pcode

with internal directories for:

vm

assembler

linker-ish/common tooling

docs

demos

Why:

p-code is a domain/subsystem, not one narrow program

the repo may become polyglot

“pcode” is the important organizing concept here

B. Pascal compiler/toolchain
Suggested repo:

sw-cor24-pascal-c

This can contain:

compiler

Pascal-oriented tooling

Pascal runtime glue if it is Pascal-specific

docs/examples/tests

This feels right because Pascal remains the primary conceptual entry point even if pieces inside touch p-code.

C. BASIC compiler/toolchain
Suggested repo:

sw-cor24-basic-c

or sw-cor24-basic-rs depending on implementation

This cleanly parallels Pascal.

Rule for suffixes
You mentioned the suffix helps you think. I think you should preserve it, but with one important rule:

Use suffixes when the repo’s main identity is an implementation artifact.
Examples:

assembler

emulator

compiler

forth implementation

Omit suffixes when the repo is more of a domain umbrella than a single implementation.
Examples:

sw-cor24-project

maybe sw-cor24-pcode if it will hold several related tools

sw-cor24-web-demos

That balance gives you clarity without making every name overly rigid.

Proposed final repo family
Here is the scheme I would recommend based on everything you said.

Umbrella / docs / landing
sw-cor24-project

Core tools
sw-cor24-assembler-rs

sw-cor24-emulator-rs

Language systems
sw-cor24-forth-a

sw-cor24-pascal-c

sw-cor24-basic-c

future:

sw-cor24-macrolisp-c

or sw-cor24-tiny-macrolisp-c if Tiny remains important there

p-code substrate
sw-cor24-pcode

Browser / demos
sw-cor24-web-demos

That is coherent.

Migration phases
Your proposed phases are good, but I would tighten and renumber them because you had two phase 2s.

Phase 1 — establish the umbrella
Goal: create the conceptual center before changing everything else.

Create:

org

sw-cor24-project

top-level README

repo index

relationship diagrams

links to existing repos

links to live demos

Deliverables
stable landing page

explanation of naming policy

disclaimer that these are independent Softwarewrighter projects targeting MakerLisp COR24

migration tracker table

Why this first
It gives users one place to follow the reorg, even while repo names are in flux.

Phase 2 — define canonical names
Goal: stop ambiguity before moving things around.

Make a table mapping:

Old repo	New canonical repo	Notes
cor24-rs	sw-cor24-assembler-rs + sw-cor24-emulator-rs	split
tf24a	sw-cor24-forth-a	renamed
p24c	sw-cor24-pascal-c	clarified
p24p	folded into sw-cor24-pcode and/or sw-cor24-pascal-c	migration
Also define:

suffix meanings

Tiny naming rules

when a domain umbrella omits the suffix

Why this matters
Without a canonical map, the migration becomes emotional and inconsistent.

Phase 3 — consolidate related repos
Goal: reduce repo sprawl where the conceptual grouping is stronger than the historical split.

You already identified the right grouping:

3A. sw-cor24-pcode
Contains:

p-code VM

p-code assembler

p-code common tooling

maybe runtime pieces that are shared substrate

3B. sw-cor24-pascal-c
Contains:

Pascal compiler

Pascal-specific tools

Pascal docs/examples/tests

3C. sw-cor24-basic-c
Contains:

BASIC compiler/tools

Benefits
fewer repos

better discoverability

less cross-repo breakage

easier browser demo linking

Risks
short-term churn

temporary duplication while you merge histories or copy code

possible confusion about what lives where

Mitigation
Use sw-cor24-project as the source of truth during the transition.

Phase 4 — move repos into the umbrella org
Goal: align hosting with the new conceptual model.

Move only after:

canonical names are documented

README redirects are ready

live-demo links are known

build/test references are inventoried

Why after consolidation
Moving before you know the final grouping creates extra churn.

Phase 5 — sweep dependencies, CI, docs, demos
Goal: make the new structure real.

Update:

README links

cross-repo docs

CI badges

GitHub Pages/live demo links

issue templates

package names if any

build scripts

workspace references

screenshots and blog links

This phase is boring but essential. It is where migrations either feel polished or broken.

Recommended migration metadata file
In sw-cor24-project, add a file like:

repos:
  - old: cor24-rs
    new:
      - sw-cor24-assembler-rs
      - sw-cor24-emulator-rs
    status: planned-split
    notes: historical combined assembler/emulator repo

  - old: tf24a
    new:
      - sw-cor24-forth-a
    status: planned-rename
    notes: Tiny Forth for COR24 in assembler; intended to grow beyond tiny

  - old: p24c
    new:
      - sw-cor24-pascal-c
    status: planned-rename
    notes: Pascal compiler in C

  - old: p24p
    new:
      - sw-cor24-pcode
      - sw-cor24-pascal-c
    status: planned-consolidation
    notes: historical naming confusion around p-code vs Pascal
This is useful for:

README generation

migration tracking

blog posts

future automation

Low-level naming recommendations
Suffixes
I would standardize these:

Suffix	Meaning
-rs	Rust
-c	C
-a	assembler
none	umbrella/domain repo
I would avoid mixing:

-asm

-s

-fth

-pas

unless you truly need them later.

Word choice
Prefer:

pcode over p-code in repo names

web-demos over just web

assembler and emulator instead of compressed names

That improves searchability and reduces guesswork.

Suggested structure of sw-cor24-project
The landing repo should have sections like:

1. What this is
Softwarewrighter’s independent COR24-targeting projects.

2. Why this exists
Your three motivations:

vibe-coding improvement

full FPGA-hosted dev toolchain

browser education/blogging/vlogging

3. Repo map
Canonical repo table.

4. Migration status
Old name → new name → status.

5. Live demos
Each demo with:

description

source repo

live URL

6. Architecture relationships
assembler/emulator

pcode substrate

Pascal

BASIC

Forth

Macro Lisp

7. Naming policy
Short explicit explanation of sw-, cor24, and suffixes.

That repo becomes both the public map and your private sanity-preserver.

Concrete recommendations on the specific names you mentioned
Keep / adopt
sw-cor24-assembler-rs

sw-cor24-emulator-rs

sw-cor24-forth-a

sw-cor24-pascal-c

Strongly consider
sw-cor24-pcode

sw-cor24-basic-c

sw-cor24-project

sw-cor24-web-demos

Avoid
cor24-c

cor24-rs

p24c

p24p

They are either ambiguous, too compressed, or too close to implying canonical/official naming.

My strongest opinion
The most important design decision here is not the exact repo names. It is this:

Make the migration concept-first, not history-first.
In other words:

do not preserve old accidental structure just because it existed

group by the system you want people to understand:

core tooling

pcode substrate

Pascal

BASIC

Forth

demos

use implementation suffixes only where they help orientation

That will give you a cleaner long-term ecosystem than trying to make p24p, p24c, and other historical names line up through clever explanation.

Recommended final plan
Phase order
Create sw-cor24-project with README, migration table, and live demo links.

Define canonical names for all repos.

Split cor24-rs into sw-cor24-assembler-rs and sw-cor24-emulator-rs.

Rename tf24a → sw-cor24-forth-a.

Rename/consolidate Pascal and pcode repos into:

sw-cor24-pcode

sw-cor24-pascal-c

sw-cor24-basic-c

Move repos under umbrella org.

Sweep docs/build/tests/links/demos until no stale names remain.

That sequence minimizes user confusion and gives you one stable landing place from the start.

The next most useful thing to produce is a canonical repo mapping table plus a first draft of the sw-cor24-project README.


I need more help/clarity on the suffixes.  I have code written in C, cor24-assembler, Rust, Pascal (e.g. its runtime), I still get confused thinking about p-code.  It has its own p-code assembler, but that was for testing, and for parts of the Pascal runtime that cannot be expressed in Pascal, so maybe never used as a suffix?  I do not plan to extend this to examples or follow-on apps written in sw-cor24-macrolisp, -forth, -pascal, -basic, -apl etc.  So maybe I just need to drop the suffixes and have a really good table somewhere to help me remember. The tools running in FPGA story is incomplete because C does not run there (yet, I think, I'm so confused).  My plan was to create the OS in my macro-lisp plus assembler. So maybe I do not need to port C from Rust to C yet.

You are really deciding what the names are for.

That is the root of the confusion.

Restated design problem
You currently have several overlapping dimensions:

target: COR24

brand: sw-

domain/tool role: assembler, emulator, macrolisp, forth, pcode, pascal, basic

implementation language: Rust, C, assembler, Pascal

execution substrate: native host, COR24, p-code VM, browser

strategic intent: educational, experimental, fuller system, bootstrap tool

If repo names try to encode too many of those at once, they become hard to reason about and easy to misread.

So the main architectural question is:

What belongs in the repo name, and what belongs in documentation?
My recommendation now is:

Drop implementation-language suffixes from most repo names
I think you are right to reconsider them.

Use repo names for:

brand

target

concept / role

Put implementation language, execution mode, maturity, and bootstrapping notes into a canonical table in the umbrella repo.

That is the cleanest model.

Why suffixes are hurting more than helping
At first, suffixes like -rs, -c, -a seem useful because they provide quick memory cues.

But in your ecosystem they break down because the reality is messier:

one project may contain multiple languages

a project may be implemented in C but target p-code

Pascal runtime pieces may be written partly in Pascal and partly in p-code assembler

something may start in Rust and later move partly to C or assembler

“where it runs” is often more important than “what it is written in”

p-code is not an implementation language, it is a VM instruction layer / intermediate representation / execution substrate

So a suffix gives a false sense of precision.

Example
sw-cor24-pascal-c

What does that actually tell you?

Pascal compiler written in C?

Pascal system with a C runtime?

Pascal compiler for COR24 that emits C?

repository mostly C today, but later mixed?

It helps a little, but it also misleads.

The deeper conceptual distinction
You are mixing up at least three different kinds of “language”:

1. Implementation language
What the repo is written in:

Rust

C

assembler

Pascal

2. Source language
What the tool processes:

Pascal compiler processes Pascal

Forth interpreter processes Forth

Lisp system processes Lisp

3. Target / execution form
What it emits or runs on:

COR24 machine code

p-code

browser demo

FPGA-hosted runtime

These are separate columns, not something you want compressed into a suffix.

Recommendation: use semantic repo names, not implementation names
Preferred repo-name pattern
Use:

sw-cor24-<domain>

Examples:

sw-cor24-project

sw-cor24-assembler

sw-cor24-emulator

sw-cor24-macrolisp

sw-cor24-forth

sw-cor24-pcode

sw-cor24-pascal

sw-cor24-basic

sw-cor24-apl

sw-cor24-web-demos

That is much easier to think about.

These names answer the most important public question:

What is this repo about?

They do not try to answer:

what language is it written in

whether it runs on FPGA yet

whether it emits p-code or machine code

whether it is tiny/full/experimental

whether it is bootstrapping or self-hosting yet

Those belong in documentation.

What should replace the suffixes?
A good authoritative table in sw-cor24-project.

I think your intuition there is exactly right.

The hub repo becomes your memory system
Instead of encoding every nuance in names, create a table like this:

Repo	Purpose	Source language / user model	Implemented in	Emits / runs on	Runs where today	Long-term direction
sw-cor24-assembler	assemble COR24 source	COR24 asm	Rust	COR24 binaries	host, browser demo	maybe more self-hosting later
sw-cor24-emulator	emulate COR24 board/system	n/a	Rust	executes COR24 binaries	host, browser demo	FPGA-adjacent teaching/demo tool
sw-cor24-macrolisp	macro-lisp system	Lisp	C + asm + Lisp	COR24/native forms	host now, COR24 later	basis for OS/tooling
sw-cor24-forth	Forth system	Forth	assembler	direct/native behavior	emulator/hardware	fuller Forth
sw-cor24-pcode	p-code VM + tools	p-code / VM substrate	mixed	executes p-code	host, emulator, browser	substrate for Pascal/BASIC
sw-cor24-pascal	Pascal compiler/toolchain	Pascal	C + Pascal + p-code parts	p-code	host now	fuller embedded Pascal
sw-cor24-basic	BASIC compiler/toolchain	BASIC	TBD	p-code	planned	teaching + embedded use
That table is far more truthful than a suffix.

How to think about p-code
This is one of the main sources of confusion, so it helps to make it explicit.

p-code should not usually be treated like a suffix category
Because p-code is not primarily:

a source language

an implementation language

a branding axis

It is more like:

a VM instruction set

an intermediate representation

a runtime substrate

a testing/bootstrapping layer

So I would not use -p or -pcode as a suffix meaning “written in p-code,” unless you truly have a repo whose primary identity is “this is a p-code program.”

For your case, it sounds like:

sw-cor24-pcode is the subsystem repo

inside it live:

VM

p-code assembler

maybe test programs

low-level runtime pieces

That is enough.

You do not need suffixes like:

-p

-pcode

-vmcode

etc.

except when pcode is the actual repo subject.

Suggested mental model
Think of your repos as belonging to one of four categories.

Category A: platform tooling
assembler

emulator

Names:

sw-cor24-assembler

sw-cor24-emulator

Category B: language systems
macrolisp

forth

pascal

basic

apl

Names:

sw-cor24-macrolisp

sw-cor24-forth

sw-cor24-pascal

sw-cor24-basic

sw-cor24-apl

Category C: runtime substrate
p-code VM

p-code assembler

shared runtime-ish pieces

Name:

sw-cor24-pcode

Category D: umbrella / docs / demos
project map

browser demos

Names:

sw-cor24-project

sw-cor24-web-demos

That is a coherent architecture.

What to do about “Tiny”
Now that you are thinking of dropping suffixes, I would also revisit where “Tiny” belongs.

My suggestion
Use “Tiny” as a display/documentation concept, not necessarily always in repo names.

For example, in the table:

Repo	Display name	Notes
sw-cor24-macrolisp	Tiny Macro Lisp for COR24	educational, small, bootstrap-oriented
sw-cor24-forth	Forth for COR24	potentially fuller system
sw-cor24-pascal	Pascal for COR24	Turbo-Pascal-inspired embedded system
This gives you freedom.

If you later decide Tiny still matters enough for some repos, you can keep it for a few:

sw-cor24-tiny-c

sw-cor24-tiny-macrolisp

But I would not force it everywhere.

On the FPGA-hosted story
You also raised an important architectural confusion:

The tools running in FPGA story is incomplete because C does not run there yet.

That is fine. Do not let the naming imply a completed self-hosting story that does not exist yet.

This is exactly why names should stay conceptual.

Repo names should describe the subsystem
not the full current or future bootstrap state.

For example:

sw-cor24-macrolisp is fine whether it currently runs:

on the host,

on the emulator,

partially on real hardware later.

Same for:

sw-cor24-pascal

sw-cor24-pcode

The “runs where” and “self-hosting status” should be table columns, not naming conventions.

Your C question
You said:

So maybe I do not need to port C from Rust to C yet.

I think that is right.

Architectural recommendation
Do not reorganize naming around a future implementation-port that may never matter.

Instead, name repos by what they are, and document:

current implementation language

current runtime environment

planned future migration, if any

That avoids premature naming decisions based on uncertain technical evolution.

If your long-term OS is really centered on macro-lisp + assembler, then C may be:

a bootstrap tool

a stepping stone

a host-side compiler

but not the center of the final story

That is another reason not to overemphasize implementation language in repo names.

Proposed final naming approach
Strong recommendation
Use these names:

sw-cor24-project

sw-cor24-assembler

sw-cor24-emulator

sw-cor24-macrolisp

sw-cor24-forth

sw-cor24-pcode

sw-cor24-pascal

sw-cor24-basic

sw-cor24-apl

sw-cor24-web-demos

Then in sw-cor24-project, maintain a canonical table with columns like:

repo

display name

purpose

source language / user-facing language

implementation language(s)

output / target

runs where today

intended future

live demo

status

This gives you clarity without forcing misleading suffixes.

If you absolutely want a lightweight implementation cue
If dropping suffixes entirely feels too lossy, use topics/tags, not names.

For example, GitHub topics like:

rust

c

assembler

pascal

pcode

cor24

fpga

emulator

compiler

lisp

forth

That gives you fast filtering without baking unstable details into repo names.

You can also put a short subtitle at the top of each README:

“Main implementation: Rust”

“Main implementation: C + Pascal + p-code support code”

“Main implementation: assembler”

That is a better place for the detail.

Decision rule
Here is the simplest rule set I would adopt.

Put in the repo name
your brand: sw

target: cor24

subsystem name: assembler, emulator, macrolisp, forth, pcode, pascal, basic

Put in the umbrella table
implementation language(s)

emits machine code or p-code

runs on host, emulator, browser, FPGA

bootstrapping status

tiny/full/experimental notes

dependency relationships

Put in README front matter
one-line summary

status

implementation languages

current execution environments

live demo link

That is enough.

My bottom-line answer
Yes — I think you should probably drop the suffixes.

Not because they were a bad idea, but because your ecosystem has grown past the point where one suffix can accurately summarize a repo.

Use clear semantic repo names and a really good central table in sw-cor24-project.

That will likely reduce confusion more than any suffix scheme.

My preferred final set
sw-cor24-project

sw-cor24-assembler

sw-cor24-emulator

sw-cor24-macrolisp

sw-cor24-forth

sw-cor24-pcode

sw-cor24-pascal

sw-cor24-basic

sw-cor24-apl

sw-cor24-web-demos

And the key insight
Let repo names answer “what subsystem is this?”
Let the table answer “how is it implemented, what does it emit, where does it run, and where is it going?”

The next useful step is to design that canonical table schema and the short naming policy for sw-cor24-project.


Another consideration (a wrench in the gears of progress?).  cor24 is just a starting point. I also plan to build FPGA soft cpus for IBM 1130, RCA 1802, IBM S/370, and IBM S/390-subset.  I plan to port these cor24 tools to those ISAs/FPGAs/emulators (to be written).  So I have an org sw-embed which covers all of these in some sense.  org sw-cor24 would isolate one set.  then I could add something like org sw-s1130 for all the 1130-related  emulator, assembler, compilers, runtimes, VMs, etc.  BTW, to broaden this, I am pivoting my branding to also include owner hardwarewrighter and hw-cor24 which will have my port of makerlisp's soft cpu from his preferred FPGA to mine.  So all my soft cpus will be under hw-s1130, hw-r1802, hw-s370, hw-s390 etc

This is not a wrench in the gears. It is the missing top-level architecture.

Restated requirements
You are no longer designing just a COR24 repo family. You are designing a multi-ISA ecosystem with at least two distinct brands:

sw-* for software toolchains, languages, runtimes, VMs, demos, and educational systems

hw-* for hardware/FPGA/soft-CPU ports and board-level work

And COR24 is only the first instance. Future families include at least:

IBM 1130

RCA 1802

IBM S/370

IBM S/390 subset

So the real design problem is now:

What is the correct hierarchy?
I think the hierarchy should be:

Brand level
sw-* = software ecosystems

hw-* = hardware ecosystems

ISA/family level
cor24

s1130

r1802

s370

s390

Project/subsystem level
assembler

emulator

pcode

pascal

forth

macrolisp

demos

runtime

fpga-core

board-port

etc.

That means you should stop trying to solve this as a single-org naming problem first. The main thing to solve is the portfolio structure.

High-level recommendation
I would not make sw-cor24 your primary umbrella org.

I would instead use:

sw-embed as the software umbrella across all embedded / retro / custom ISA toolchains

hardwarewrighter or a hardware umbrella brand as the hardware counterpart

Then under those umbrellas, use repo naming conventions that encode ISA family.

That gives you a clean two-axis model:

Software side
org: sw-embed

repos:

sw-cor24-project

sw-cor24-assembler

sw-cor24-emulator

sw-cor24-pascal

sw-s1130-project

sw-s1130-assembler

sw-r1802-project

sw-s370-project

Hardware side
org: hardwarewrighter or hw-embed if you want symmetry

repos:

hw-cor24

hw-s1130

hw-r1802

hw-s370

hw-s390

That is much more future-proof than creating a separate org per ISA right away.

Why separate org-per-ISA is probably the wrong first move
You proposed something like:

sw-cor24

sw-s1130

sw-s370

etc.

That can work, but I think it is too fragmented for where you are now.

Pros
very clean conceptual isolation

easy mental separation by ISA family

each org can look self-contained

Cons
too many orgs too early

repeated profile README/admin/config overhead

harder cross-family discoverability

harder to show the shared grand vision

more painful if projects span families

Since you already know these families are conceptually related, a single software umbrella org is probably better.

My recommendation
Use repos for ISA partitioning, not orgs, at least initially.

So:

one software umbrella org: sw-embed

one hardware umbrella org: hardwarewrighter or hw-embed

Then inside each, partition by repo names.

Best overall structure
Option A — recommended
Software org
sw-embed

Hardware org
hardwarewrighter

or hw-embed if you want stronger symmetry

Repo naming
Software:

sw-cor24-project

sw-cor24-assembler

sw-cor24-emulator

sw-cor24-macrolisp

sw-cor24-forth

sw-cor24-pcode

sw-cor24-pascal

sw-cor24-basic

Future software:

sw-s1130-project

sw-s1130-assembler

sw-s1130-emulator

sw-r1802-project

sw-s370-project

sw-s390-project

Hardware:

hw-cor24

hw-s1130

hw-r1802

hw-s370

hw-s390

This is the cleanest structure.

Option B — acceptable but more fragmented
Separate orgs by ISA family:

Software:

sw-cor24

sw-s1130

sw-r1802

sw-s370

sw-s390

Hardware:

hw-cor24

hw-s1130

hw-r1802

hw-s370

hw-s390

I would only do this later, after one or two families are mature enough that each deserves its own public front door.

Why the two-brand model is actually very strong
You said:

hw-cor24 = your hardware port of MakerLisp’s soft CPU to your FPGA

sw-cor24-* = your software toolchain and language ecosystem targeting COR24

That is excellent separation.

It gives you clean semantics
hw-*
Means:

FPGA core

board adaptation

HDL

timing/resource issues

pin constraints

board-specific integration

maybe peripheral support

sw-*
Means:

assembler

emulator

compilers

runtimes

VMs

languages

browser demos

educational tooling

operating system experiments

That division is much clearer than mixing “hardware port” and “language toolchain” in one namespace.

Recommended portfolio architecture
Think of the full portfolio as four layers.

Layer 1 — brand umbrellas
sw-embed

hardwarewrighter or hw-embed

Layer 2 — ISA families
COR24

S1130

R1802

S370

S390

Layer 3 — subsystem repos
Software:

project

assembler

emulator

macrolisp

forth

pcode

pascal

basic

apl

web-demos

Hardware:

soft-cpu

fpga-port

board-support

peripherals

demos

Layer 4 — apps/examples/content
These probably do not need separate top-level repos yet.

Naming recommendation after this new information
This new information strengthens the case for:

Keep cor24 in repo names
because it is the ISA family discriminator.

But:

Do not make sw-cor24 the master org
because COR24 is only one family.

So the right shape is:

Org
sw-embed

Repos
sw-cor24-project

sw-cor24-assembler

sw-cor24-emulator

sw-cor24-macrolisp

sw-cor24-forth

sw-cor24-pcode

sw-cor24-pascal

Then later:

sw-s1130-project

sw-s1130-assembler

sw-r1802-project

sw-s370-project

sw-s390-project

This is exactly the sort of repo-family pattern that scales.

What sw-cor24-project becomes in this bigger picture
It is no longer “the big umbrella for everything.”

It becomes:

a family hub repo
for the COR24 branch of the broader sw-embed software ecosystem.

That is good. It reduces pressure on it.

Then eventually you may also want:

sw-embed-project

which would explain the whole multi-ISA vision:

custom and historical ISAs

compilers and runtimes

browser demos

FPGA-hosted tools

OS experiments

cross-family porting

So the structure could become:

At org level
sw-embed

Top portfolio map
sw-embed-project

Family hubs
sw-cor24-project

sw-s1130-project

sw-r1802-project

sw-s370-project

sw-s390-project

This is very strong architecture.

Same idea on the hardware side
If you are serious about hardwarewrighter / hw-*, I would mirror the pattern.

Hardware org
Either:

hardwarewrighter
or

hw-embed

Portfolio map
hw-embed-project or hardwarewrighter-project

Family repos
hw-cor24

hw-s1130

hw-r1802

hw-s370

hw-s390

You could also split hardware further later if needed:

hw-cor24-core

hw-cor24-board-port

hw-cor24-peripherals

But for now I would keep hardware family repos broad.

Clear separation of responsibility
Here is the most useful decision table.

Namespace	Meaning	Examples
sw-embed	software umbrella org	toolchains, runtimes, VMs, compilers, demos
sw-<isa>-*	software for a specific ISA family	sw-cor24-pascal, sw-s370-emulator
hardwarewrighter / hw-embed	hardware umbrella org	HDL, FPGA ports, boards
hw-<isa>	hardware family repo	hw-cor24, hw-s1130
That is clean and extensible.

Your concern about COR24 ownership/branding
This new structure also solves that better.

If you use:

org: sw-embed

repo: sw-cor24-*

then the name says:

these are softwarewrighter projects

they target COR24

they are not claiming the COR24 brand itself

Likewise:

hw-cor24 means your hardware/FPGA work around COR24, not the official COR24 project.

You should still put a disclaimer near the top of the README, but the naming becomes much less problematic.

What I would avoid
Avoid this for now
a separate org for each ISA

suffixes for implementation language in repo names

making cor24 the umbrella for work that is really multi-ISA

mixing hardware and software under one repo family without clear labeling

Why
Because your actual vision is broader:

multiple ISAs

multiple tools

software plus hardware

long-term OS/toolchain/browser story

The naming should match the long-term architecture, not just the current first family.

Recommended immediate plan
Phase 0 — decide the umbrella brands
Make a final call on:

Software
sw-embed as main software org

Hardware
hardwarewrighter or hw-embed as hardware org

I slightly prefer:

sw-embed

hardwarewrighter

because they reflect your existing and emerging branding.

Phase 1 — create the software umbrella hub
Create:

sw-embed-project

This explains:

overall mission

software families by ISA

relationship to hardwarewrighter / hw-*

portfolio map

Then create:

sw-cor24-project

This explains the COR24-specific family.

Phase 2 — establish COR24 family repos
Use semantic names:

sw-cor24-assembler

sw-cor24-emulator

sw-cor24-macrolisp

sw-cor24-forth

sw-cor24-pcode

sw-cor24-pascal

sw-cor24-web-demos

No implementation-language suffixes.

Phase 3 — establish hardware family repos
Create:

hw-cor24

later:

hw-s1130

hw-r1802

hw-s370

hw-s390

Phase 4 — document the cross-links
Every sw-cor24-* repo should link to:

sw-cor24-project

related hw-cor24 repo when relevant

Every hw-cor24 repo should link to:

sw-cor24-project

the relevant software tools

This reinforces the two-brand model.

My strongest recommendation now
Given everything you have said, I would choose this structure:

Software umbrella org
sw-embed

software portfolio hub
sw-embed-project

COR24 family
sw-cor24-project

sw-cor24-assembler

sw-cor24-emulator

sw-cor24-macrolisp

sw-cor24-forth

sw-cor24-pcode

sw-cor24-pascal

sw-cor24-basic

sw-cor24-apl

sw-cor24-web-demos

future families
sw-s1130-project

sw-r1802-project

sw-s370-project

sw-s390-project

Hardware umbrella org
hardwarewrighter
or hw-embed

hardware family repos
hw-cor24

hw-s1130

hw-r1802

hw-s370

hw-s390

That gives you a scalable portfolio and avoids painting yourself into a COR24-only corner.

Bottom line
This new information makes the direction clearer:

Do not make COR24 the top-level umbrella.
Make it one family inside a broader software umbrella.

Use orgs for brand/domain boundaries.
software = sw-embed

hardware = hardwarewrighter or hw-embed

Use repos for ISA families and subsystems.
sw-cor24-*

sw-s1130-*

hw-cor24

hw-s370

That is the architecture most likely to survive the next several years of growth.

The next thing worth drafting is a two-level portfolio map:

sw-embed-project top-level structure

sw-cor24-project family structure


well the LLC Softwarewrighter is the umbrella brand (owner, company, top level "org").  I like your suggestions.  I can make hardwarewrighter an org under softwarerighter (the github owner).  This makes a lot of sense to me (and is simpler than introducting another "top" level github owner with all the permissions/keys overhead that entails. 


Thought for 8s
Yes — that is a cleaner model.

Recommended structure
Use:

GitHub personal account / owner: softwarewrighter

Software org: sw-embed

Hardware org: hardwarewrighter

Then keep the repo families inside those orgs:

In sw-embed
sw-embed-project

sw-cor24-project

sw-cor24-assembler

sw-cor24-emulator

sw-cor24-macrolisp

sw-cor24-forth

sw-cor24-pcode

sw-cor24-pascal

In hardwarewrighter
hw-cor24

later:

hw-s1130

hw-r1802

hw-s370

hw-s390

Important GitHub nuance
A GitHub organization is not actually nested under a personal account or another organization in the ownership sense. GitHub’s account types are separate: personal accounts, organization accounts, and enterprise accounts. Organizations are shared accounts; people sign in with personal accounts and can own or administer organizations. If you want true central management of multiple organizations, that is the role of a GitHub enterprise account, not one org “inside” another. 

So the practical interpretation of your idea is:

softwarewrighter is your main identity / top-level brand

hardwarewrighter is a separate org you control

both can coexist without introducing another unrelated top-level brand

you manage them from your personal/admin access, rather than by literally nesting one under the other. 

Why this is a good fit
This gives you:

one clear company/owner identity: softwarewrighter

one clear software umbrella: sw-embed

one clear hardware umbrella: hardwarewrighter

That matches your actual architecture:

software ecosystems span multiple ISA families

hardware ecosystems span multiple FPGA soft CPUs and board ports

COR24 becomes just one family among several, rather than the top-level container

My strongest recommendation
I would treat the hierarchy like this conceptually:

Softwarewrighter (company / owner / main brand)
├── sw-embed          # software org
│   ├── sw-embed-project
│   ├── sw-cor24-project
│   ├── sw-s1130-project
│   └── ...
└── hardwarewrighter  # hardware org
    ├── hw-cor24
    ├── hw-s1130
    └── ...
Even though GitHub won’t model that as literal nested ownership, it works very well as a branding and portfolio structure. GitHub organizations are intended as branded containers for shared work, and enterprise accounts are the layer above organizations if you ever need centralized org governance later. 

Suggested next move
Start with just these three anchors:

softwarewrighter as the public parent brand

sw-embed for software repos

hardwarewrighter for hardware repos

Then create:

sw-embed-project

sw-cor24-project

hw-cor24

That gives you a stable structure without overcommitting to too many orgs or names at once.

content.text:
That's a comprehensive research document capturing a multi-session planning conversation about organizing your COR24 and broader embedded systems project ecosystem. Here's the key takeaways:

**Final recommended structure:**

- **Owner/brand:** `softwarewrighter` (your LLC)
- **Software org:** `sw-embed` — all software toolchains across ISA families
- **Hardware org:** `hardwarewrighter` — all FPGA/soft-CPU work

**COR24 software repos** (under `sw-embed`):
| Repo | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `sw-embed-project` | Top-level portfolio hub |
| `sw-cor24-project` | COR24 family hub |
| `sw-cor24-assembler` | Assembler (currently Rust) |
| `sw-cor24-emulator` | Emulator (currently Rust) |
| `sw-cor24-macrolisp` | Macro Lisp system |
| `sw-cor24-forth` | Forth system |
| `sw-cor24-pcode` | p-code VM + assembler + tools |
| `sw-cor24-pascal` | Pascal compiler/runtime/linker |
| `sw-cor24-basic` | BASIC (future) |
| `sw-cor24-web-demos` | Browser demos |

**Key decisions:**
- No implementation-language suffixes (drop `-rs`, `-c`, `-a`) — use a canonical table instead
- "Tiny" used as display name concept, not in repo names
- COR24 is one ISA family; future families: S1130, R1802, S370, S390
- 5-phase migration: umbrella → canonical names → consolidate → move repos → sweep deps/docs

**Migration phases** map old repos (`cor24-rs`, `tf24a`, `p24c`, `p24p`) to new canonical names.
